description: First-On-Scene deterministic detection ruleset. Each rule maps to a MITRE ATT&CK technique. The analyzer evaluates these generically against an artifact bundle. Authoring guide: docs/RULES.md.

scoring.notes: verdict = ALL_CLEAR (<monitor) | MONITOR (>=monitor, <problem) | PROBLEM_DETECTED (>=problem). classification escalates to Breach at >=breach OR when any matched rule sets escalateToBreach=true.

rules.name: Registry Run key launching from a non-system path
rules.rationale: Autorun entries that execute binaries from user, temp, or AppData paths are a classic persistence mechanism.
rules.falsePositive: Some legitimate per-user apps (updaters, sync clients) register Run keys under AppData. Confirm the publisher and signature of the target binary.

rules.name: Scheduled task executing from a user/temp path
rules.rationale: Scheduled tasks pointing at user-writable directories and not authored by Microsoft are frequently used for persistence and re-infection.
rules.falsePositive: Line-of-business apps and some installers create user-scoped tasks. Validate the action target and signing.

rules.name: Auto-start service from a non-standard path
rules.rationale: Auto-start services running executables outside Windows/Program Files are a high-confidence persistence indicator.
rules.falsePositive: A few third-party agents install under ProgramData or custom roots. Verify the vendor.

rules.name: WMI permanent event subscription present
rules.rationale: WMI permanent event subscriptions are a fileless persistence technique rarely used by legitimate software.
rules.falsePositive: A small number of management/EDR products register WMI consumers. Enumerate the consumer command lines.

rules.name: Process running from a user/temp directory
rules.rationale: Executables launched from temporary directories are a common malware staging pattern.
rules.falsePositive: Installers and self-updating apps frequently execute from Temp. Correlate with signing, parent process, and network activity.

rules.name: LOLBin spawned from Office or script host
rules.rationale: Office or scripting hosts spawning living-off-the-land binaries is a hallmark of phishing-delivered execution.
rules.falsePositive: Rare in normal use; investigate every hit.

rules.name: Established connection to external host on a high-risk port
rules.rationale: Outbound sessions to public IPs on known C2/Metasploit/IRC ports strongly indicate command-and-control.
rules.falsePositive: 8443 is sometimes a legitimate management port. Resolve the remote IP and owning process.

rules.name: External RDP/SMB exposure (lateral movement surface)
rules.rationale: RDP or SMB sessions to/from public addresses indicate either exposure or active lateral movement.
rules.falsePositive: Site-to-site VPNs and cloud-hosted servers can produce legitimate external RDP/SMB.

rules.name: PowerShell download cradle / in-memory execution
rules.rationale: Download cradles and in-memory code execution (IEX + Net.WebClient.DownloadString, reflective assembly loads) are high-confidence indicators of malicious PowerShell.
rules.falsePositive: A few legitimate bootstrap/installer scripts use Invoke-Expression. Read the full script block for the URL and intent.

rules.name: Encoded PowerShell command
rules.rationale: Base64/encoded PowerShell can hide intent. Low weight on its own because legitimate RMM and automation tooling (including NinjaOne) routinely use -EncodedCommand.
rules.falsePositive: Very common in RMM/automation. Treat as corroborating context, not a standalone indicator.

rules.name: Privileged logon correlated with service logon
rules.rationale: Co-occurrence of special-privilege logons (4672) and service logons (4624 type 5) can indicate token abuse or malicious service installation.
rules.falsePositive: Normal on busy servers. Treat as corroborating, not standalone, evidence.

rules.name: New local user account created
rules.rationale: Account creation (4720) outside a known provisioning workflow is a persistence and backdoor indicator.
rules.falsePositive: Legitimate onboarding/IT provisioning. Correlate with change tickets.

rules.name: Timestomping (creation time later than last write)
rules.rationale: A creation timestamp later than the last-write timestamp is a strong anti-forensic timestomping indicator.
rules.falsePositive: File copy/restore operations and some backup tools can legitimately reorder MACE times.

rules.name: Windows Defender detected threats
rules.rationale: An on-box AV engine confirming a detection is high-confidence evidence of malware.
rules.falsePositive: PUA/heuristic detections occasionally over-report. Confirm the threat name.

rules.name: ClamAV detected infected files
rules.rationale: ClamAV signature hits indicate known-malicious files present on disk.
rules.falsePositive: ClamAV can flag cracked software and test files (EICAR). Verify the signature name.

rules.name: Ransom note filename indicator
rules.rationale: Ransom-note filenames in collected file metadata are a near-certain ransomware impact indicator.
rules.falsePositive: A plain README.txt is benign; the regex targets recovery/decrypt phrasing. Confirm contents.

rules.name: Cron job invoking a download cradle or shell
rules.rationale: Cron entries that fetch and pipe code to a shell, or run from /tmp, are a Linux persistence/backdoor pattern.
rules.falsePositive: Some legitimate maintenance jobs use curl/wget. Review the full command.

rules.name: systemd service executing from a world-writable path
rules.rationale: systemd units launching binaries from /tmp, /dev/shm, or hidden home paths are a strong Linux persistence indicator.
rules.falsePositive: Uncommon for legitimate units. Investigate every hit.

rules.name: Remote-access / RMM tool present but not declared
rules.rationale: A remote-access or RMM agent is present that was not listed in -ExpectedRemoteTools. In an MSP context this is almost always one of your own sanctioned agents, so it is shown for review only and does NOT affect the verdict. Confirm each tool is yours; an agent you genuinely did not install (e.g. attacker-staged AnyDesk, TeamViewer, or RustDesk) is worth investigating.
rules.falsePositive: Almost always a legitimate tool the MSP did not declare. List your sanctioned agents with -ExpectedRemoteTools (e.g. 'NinjaOne,ScreenConnect') to label them as expected.

rules.name: Expected remote-access tool present (context)
rules.rationale: Sanctioned remote-access / RMM agents found on the host, shown for context. Does not affect the score.
rules.falsePositive: Informational only.

rules.name: Microsoft Defender real-time protection is OFF
rules.rationale: Disabled real-time AV protection on a suspected-compromise host is a top attacker action (impair defenses).
rules.falsePositive: A third-party AV may legitimately suppress Defender. Confirm another AV product is active and managed.

rules.name: Defender exclusions are configured
rules.rationale: Attackers add path/extension/process exclusions so their tooling is never scanned. Review every exclusion.
rules.falsePositive: Many environments set legitimate exclusions (line-of-business apps, backup agents). Compare against your baseline.

rules.name: Defender tamper protection is OFF
rules.rationale: Tamper protection off allows AV settings to be silently changed; a common pre-attack step.
rules.falsePositive: Some management tooling disables it. Verify it is intentional and policy-managed.

rules.name: Unsigned executable running from a user/temp path
rules.rationale: A process with no valid Authenticode signature running from a user-writable path is a strong malware indicator.
rules.falsePositive: Some legitimate niche/in-house tools are unsigned. Check the SHA-256 against your tooling or an IOC source.

rules.name: Running process whose on-disk image was deleted
rules.rationale: A process still running while its executable has been deleted from disk is a classic drop-execute-delete malware pattern.
rules.falsePositive: Can occur right after a legitimate app self-update. Correlate with signing and network activity.

rules.name: Unsigned module loaded into a process (possible injection)
rules.rationale: An unsigned DLL loaded from a user/temp path into another process can indicate DLL injection or side-loading (-Deep scan).
rules.falsePositive: Some legitimate plugins are unsigned. Verify the module path and publisher.

rules.name: Image File Execution Options debugger hijack
rules.rationale: An IFEO 'Debugger' value silently launches an attacker program whenever a target executable runs.
rules.falsePositive: Legitimate debuggers/AV occasionally set IFEO. Inspect the debugger target.

rules.name: AppInit_DLLs is populated
rules.rationale: AppInit_DLLs are loaded into nearly every process; attackers use it for system-wide injection/persistence.
rules.falsePositive: Rare in modern, well-managed systems. Investigate the listed DLL.

rules.name: Winlogon Shell/Userinit modified
rules.rationale: Winlogon Shell/Userinit deviating from defaults runs attacker code at every interactive logon.
rules.falsePositive: Some legacy line-of-business shells customize these. Verify the value.

rules.name: Accessibility binary hijack (sticky-keys backdoor)
rules.rationale: An IFEO/debugger on sethc/utilman/osk (accessibility binaries) is a pre-auth backdoor reachable from the logon screen.
rules.falsePositive: Essentially always malicious. Investigate immediately.

rules.name: Security/audit log was cleared
rules.rationale: Clearing the Security or System event log (1102/104) is a deliberate anti-forensic act around the incident window.
rules.falsePositive: Rare administratively. Correlate the clear time with other activity.

rules.name: Hosts file redirects a domain to a non-loopback address
rules.rationale: A hosts-file entry mapping a real hostname to an arbitrary (non-loopback, non-null) IP can silently redirect traffic - classic for blocking AV/update domains or hijacking a site to an attacker server.
rules.falsePositive: Developers and admins legitimately pin internal hostnames to LAN IPs in the hosts file. Ad-block lists map to 0.0.0.0/127.0.0.1 and are intentionally excluded. Review each redirected host and its target IP.

rules.name: Process listening on a known bind-shell / C2 port
rules.rationale: A process bound to all interfaces on a known Metasploit/bind-shell/C2 port is a strong indicator of an implant awaiting connections.
rules.falsePositive: A few legitimate apps use high ports. Loopback-only listeners are excluded. Resolve the owning process and confirm it is expected.

rules.name: Windows Firewall profile is disabled
rules.rationale: A disabled firewall profile widens the host's exposure and is a common attacker action to enable inbound C2 or lateral movement.
rules.falsePositive: A managed third-party firewall may legitimately turn the Windows Firewall profile off. Confirm another firewall is active and policy-managed.

rules.name: Unsigned kernel driver loaded from a non-standard path
rules.rationale: A running kernel driver that is unsigned and loaded from outside system32\drivers is a rootkit / bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver indicator (-Deep scan).
rules.falsePositive: Rare. A handful of OEM/storage drivers load from vendor paths and may report as catalog-signed rather than Valid. Verify the driver vendor and SHA-256.

rules.name: Autostart / launchd entry runs from a suspicious path
rules.rationale: A macOS LaunchAgent/LaunchDaemon or Linux autostart entry whose program lives in a world-writable/temp/hidden path - or that pipes a download into a shell - is a strong cross-platform persistence indicator (macOS launchd is the dominant native persistence surface).
rules.falsePositive: A few legitimate per-user agents live under a user's home. Confirm the target binary's publisher/signature and that the plist was installed by sanctioned software.

rules.name: macOS system protection disabled (SIP / Gatekeeper)
rules.rationale: System Integrity Protection or Gatekeeper being OFF removes a core macOS defense (kernel/file protection or unsigned-code blocking) and is a common attacker or pre-attack action to impair defenses. The collector only records a control here when it is disabled.
rules.falsePositive: Developers sometimes disable Gatekeeper; SIP-disabled is rare on a managed endpoint. Confirm the change was intentional and policy-approved.

rules.name: Executable or installer downloaded from the internet (Mark-of-the-Web)
rules.rationale: A program or installer carrying a Zone.Identifier (Mark-of-the-Web) was downloaded from an external URL into a user-writable location. This is the classic delivery vector for malicious remote-access tools and loaders (e.g. a ScreenConnect/MSI dropper pulled from an attacker site).
rules.falsePositive: Users legitimately download installers. Confirm the source URL, the publisher/signature of the file, and whether the download was expected.

rules.name: File downloaded from a suspicious or low-reputation source host
rules.rationale: The download originated from a host on a high-abuse top-level domain or a bare IP address. Phishing lures and malware-delivery infrastructure overwhelmingly use these throwaway domains/IPs rather than reputable vendor sites.
rules.falsePositive: A small number of legitimate services use these TLDs. Treat as a strong lead, not proof: pivot on the full URL, the downloaded file, and the user's account of how they got the link.

rules.name: Archive recently staged in a user or temp directory
rules.rationale: An archive was written to a user-writable/temp path within the collection window. Attackers routinely compress collected files into an archive immediately before exfiltration to reduce volume and evade content inspection.
rules.falsePositive: Backups, software downloads, and user-created archives are common here. Correlate the archive's contents, size, and creation time with other indicators before treating it as staged data.

rules.name: BITS transfer job to or from an external URL
rules.rationale: A Background Intelligent Transfer Service job is moving data to/from a public URL. BITS survives reboots and logoffs and is a favored low-noise channel for both pulling additional payloads and exfiltrating data.
rules.falsePositive: Windows Update, Microsoft Store, and some management agents use BITS. Verify the owner, the remote URL's reputation, and the local file path.

rules.name: Data-egress / file-transfer tool present
rules.rationale: A utility commonly used to move bulk data off a host (rclone, MEGAcmd, FileZilla, WinSCP, croc, magic-wormhole, etc.) is installed or running. These are not default Windows components; on a workstation hit by a remote-access intrusion their presence is a candidate exfiltration mechanism.
rules.falsePositive: Admins and developers legitimately use FileZilla/WinSCP/rsync. Confirm whether the tool is sanctioned for this user and whether it ran during the incident window.

rules.name: File transfer through a remote-access tool session
rules.rationale: A remote-access/RMM tool's own logs contain file-transfer entries, indicating files were moved through an interactive remote session. When the remote tool is unauthorized (see the undeclared-RMM rule), this is the most likely channel for hands-on-keyboard data theft and warrants reviewing exactly which files moved.
rules.falsePositive: Sanctioned RMM/support tools transfer files during legitimate support. Confirm the tool is authorized and correlate the transfer timestamps with a known support session.
